item.title: Judge says child responsibility comes with legal responsibility
item.description: Lautoka High Court judge has stressed that “child responsibility and legal responsibility go hand in hand” while sentencing a grandmother whose negligence led to the death of her three-year-old grandson in Sigatoka. Delivering sentence on July 20, Justice Sunil Sharma sentenced the mother to two years imprisonment, suspended for three years, after she pleaded guilty to one count of manslaughter ar
item.content:encoded:
Lautoka High Court judge has stressed that “child responsibility and legal responsibility go hand in hand” while sentencing a grandmother whose negligence led to the death of her three-year-old grandson in Sigatoka.

Delivering sentence on July 20, Justice Sunil Sharma sentenced the mother to two years imprisonment, suspended for three years, after she pleaded guilty to one count of manslaughter arising from a breach of duty.

The court heard that on January 29 this year, she crossed a pedestrian crossing with one grandchild, leaving three-year-eight-month-old son about 10 metres behind. The child entered the crossing unattended and was struck by a vehicle, later dying from his injuries.

Justice Sharma said the loss of the child could have been avoided.

“Child responsibility and legal responsibility go hand in hand, and any breaches would have serious consequences.”

He added that while the accused would carry the pain of losing her grandchild forever, the court could not ignore that an innocent life had been lost.

“No amount of repentance will bring back the victim.”

Justice Sharma said the accused had shown genuine remorse, pleaded guilty at the earliest opportunity, cooperated fully with police and accepted responsibility for her actions.

He also noted she continued to care for her other grandchildren without incident, demonstrating that she had learned from her mistake.

The court ruled that these factors made immediate imprisonment inappropriate and justified a wholly suspended sentence.

In addition to the suspended prison term, the mother was ordered to complete two sessions of general and parental counselling through the Ministry of Women, Children and Social Protection within three months.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Strong El Niño to persist as Fiji waters cool: Met Office
item.description: A strong El Niño is expected to persist for the remainder of 2026, with the Fiji Meteorological Service forecasting cooler-than-normal sea surface temperatures across much of Fiji’s waters between August and October. In its latest Fiji Ocean Outlook , the Fiji Met Service says below-normal sea surface temperatures are likely around the Western and Central divisions, Kadavu and the southern Lau Gro
item.content:encoded:
A strong El Niño is expected to persist for the remainder of 2026, with the Fiji Meteorological Service forecasting cooler-than-normal sea surface temperatures across much of Fiji’s waters between August and October.

In its latest Fiji Ocean Outlook , the Fiji Met Service says below-normal sea surface temperatures are likely around the Western and Central divisions, Kadavu and the southern Lau Group, despite above-normal temperatures being recorded across Fiji’s waters during June.

The report says the South Pacific Convergence Zone (SPCZ) is also expected to shift south of its normal position, just outside Fiji’s Exclusive Economic Zone, a movement that can influence the distribution of skipjack tuna across the Pacific.

Despite the ongoing El Niño, the outlook brings positive news for Fiji’s reefs, with the four, eight and 12-week coral bleaching outlook remaining at “No Stress” across Fiji and Rotuma.

The Met Service also forecasts below-normal sea levels across most of the Fiji Group during the August to October period, while Rotuma is expected to experience near-normal sea levels.

The report notes that El Niño conditions are typically associated with reduced rainfall in Fiji, often increasing the likelihood of prolonged dry conditions across the country.

Read Today’s Fiji Times Read Todays Fiji Times Today's Paper Subscribe Trending Stories Investigation nears end Grower urges military deployment to save crushing season $650m debt write-off defended Former inmates urged to return to villages The walk for hope © 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Judge orders Nigerian national access to phone evidence
item.description: The High Court has ruled that a Nigerian man accused of importing nearly five kilograms of methamphetamine must be allowed to inspect the mobile phone at the centre of the prosecution’s case, saying the move is necessary to protect his right to a fair trial. In a ruling delivered on July 20, Justice Aruna Aluthge granted an application by Chukwuemeka Phabian, who sought access to the phone belongi
item.content:encoded:
The High Court has ruled that a Nigerian man accused of importing nearly five kilograms of methamphetamine must be allowed to inspect the mobile phone at the centre of the prosecution’s case, saying the move is necessary to protect his right to a fair trial.

In a ruling delivered on July 20, Justice Aruna Aluthge granted an application by Chukwuemeka Phabian, who sought access to the phone belonging to prosecution witness Viliame Nalagi, an alleged accomplice who was later granted immunity.

The prosecution intends to rely on communications extracted from Nalagi’s phone to allege that Phabian is the person identified as “Pastor Thomas” in conversations linked to the alleged importation of 4.8685 kilograms of methamphetamine. Phabian denies both the charge and that he is “Pastor Thomas.”

Justice Aluthge said the existing disclosure framework in Fiji does not adequately address electronic evidence and highlighted the importance of ensuring digital evidence can be independently verified.

He ruled that digital evidence is vulnerable to alteration and that the defence is entitled to inspect the device to test its authenticity and integrity.

“Adopting an explicit disclosure and forensic inspection framework for digital devices is crucial to prevent miscarriages of justice.”

The judge also found that the prosecution’s disclosure of extracted data on a USB did not remove its obligation to ensure the defence could properly examine the evidence.

He said denying access would prejudice the accused’s fair trial rights more than it would affect the privacy interests of the immunity witness.

However, Justice Aluthge limited the inspection to communications directly relevant to the criminal charge, saying the defence must not be allowed to conduct a “fishing expedition” through the witness’s private data.

The inspection is to be carried out at a controlled location under the supervision of the Fiji Police Digital Forensics Unit.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Monasavu beneficiaries receive payouts
item.description: ABOUT $31,000 was distributed to a faction of Monasavu landowners during the 20th cycle of distribution of a $52.8 million settlement ordered by the High Court in 2000. This week, the funds was distributed to members of the Mataqali Rovadrau in Nadarivatu while next week members of the Mataqali Nadrogo and Mataqali Nakorolo will receive their 60 per cent cut.
item.content:encoded:
ABOUT $31,000 was distributed to a faction of Monasavu landowners during the 20th cycle of distribution of a $52.8 million settlement ordered by the High Court in 2000.

This week, the funds was distributed to members of the Mataqali Rovadrau in Nadarivatu while next week members of the Mataqali Nadrogo and Mataqali Nakorolo will receive their 60 per cent cut.

The distribution of the funds was carried out by law firm Fa and Company.

According to the firm, the distribution of the lease money would be done over two to three weeks.

“A total of approximately $31,000 was prepared for disbursement (to Mataqali Rovadrau), including payments to the Public Trustee on behalf of beneficiaries under the age of 18 years,” a firm statement said.

“It was encouraging to see the enthusiasm of the members of Mataqali Rovadrau as they received their payments.

“Thank you to Team Fa & Co for your dedication and hard work in ensuring a smooth and successful first day.

“Over the next two to three weeks, distributions will continue and we will release a schedule collection timetable for each Mataqali, together with a schedule for village visits.”

A total of $52.8 million settlement was ordered by the High Court to be paid to landowners in 2000 by Energy Fiji Limited and Government. The settlement outlined that funds be paid in semi-annual installments and administered by Fa & Co over a 99 year period.

In addition, the iTaukei Lands Trust Board has been issuing water royalty since 2006 that reached $5.6 million in 2025 and $5.6 million in annual rental fees from EFL between 2006 and 2025.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: RFMF Commander calls for honest communication among military leaders
item.description: The Commander of the Republic of Fiji Military Forces, Major General Ro Jone Kalouniwai, has urged the Force’s senior leadership to embrace open, frank and candid communication, saying effective dialogue is critical to maintaining a strong and professional military. Speaking at the opening of the RFMF Veiliutaki Framework Lead Organization Group Workshop at Volivoli Beach Resort yesterday, Maj Gen
item.content:encoded:
The Commander of the Republic of Fiji Military Forces, Major General Ro Jone Kalouniwai, has urged the Force’s senior leadership to embrace open, frank and candid communication, saying effective dialogue is critical to maintaining a strong and professional military.

Speaking at the opening of the RFMF Veiliutaki Framework Lead Organization Group Workshop at Volivoli Beach Resort yesterday, Maj Gen Kalouniwai said decisions made by the Force’s senior leaders directly shape every unit of the military and reinforce the confidence the people of Fiji place in the RFMF.

He stressed that poor communication has historically been a greater cause of military failure than shortages of equipment or personnel.

“No army has ever failed for want of equipment or numbers half as often as it has failed for want of communication among its leaders,” he said.

The Commander encouraged senior officers to openly discuss issues relating to operational readiness, logistics, training, discipline and troop morale before they escalate into larger problems.

He assured officers that anyone raising concerns in good faith would not face retribution, urging leaders to foster a culture of honesty and accountability.

Maj Gen Kalouniwai also acknowledged Fiji’s evolving national landscape, saying the country is navigating constitutional debate, fiscal constraints and increased public scrutiny of institutions.

He said the RFMF must respond by becoming “the professional and modern force that the nation deserves.”

“If we model integrity, forthrightness, and the courage to be corrected, we will build a Force capable of adapting to whatever this decade in Fiji’s history asks of it,” he said.

The Commander concluded by calling on members of the Lead Organization Group to embrace honest dialogue, respectful disagreement and collective responsibility as they guide the future direction of the RFMF.

The workshop, facilitated by the Institute for Leader Development of the New Zealand Defence Force and the RFMF Veiliutaki Framework team, provides senior military leaders with an opportunity to discuss strategic priorities, strengthen institutional coordination and ensure the Force remains prepared to meet its constitutional responsibilities.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TikToker under probe
item.description: THE Ministry of Education is investigating a viral video showing an American TikTok content creator preaching at a prominent Lautoka school and singling out a Muslim student during a religious discussion. The man, who reportedly visited several secondary schools, told students only Christians would enter heaven before questioning a Muslim student about his beliefs.
item.content:encoded:
THE Ministry of Education is investigating a viral video showing an American TikTok content creator preaching at a prominent Lautoka school and singling out a Muslim student during a religious discussion.

The man, who reportedly visited several secondary schools, told students only Christians would enter heaven before questioning a Muslim student about his beliefs.

The footage has triggered public backlash, with the creator’s TikTok account being deactivated as authorities examine how the school visits were conducted.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Doctor’s alleged murder weapon produced before High Court
item.description: A cane knife allegedly used in the September 2025 alleged murder of Dr Isireli Biumaitotoya was recovered from behind an old fridge inside the victim’s home after the accused, Peceli Rosotokaicake Rokoravolo, disclosed its location. Testifying before Lautoka High Court Judge Justice Aruna Aluthge, Crime Scene Investigation team leader Detective Sergeant Gasio Rokodulu said 20 exhibits were into ev
item.content:encoded:
A cane knife allegedly used in the September 2025 alleged murder of Dr Isireli Biumaitotoya was recovered from behind an old fridge inside the victim’s home after the accused, Peceli Rosotokaicake Rokoravolo, disclosed its location.

Testifying before Lautoka High Court Judge Justice Aruna Aluthge, Crime Scene Investigation team leader Detective Sergeant Gasio Rokodulu said 20 exhibits were into evidence, including the blood-stained, wooden-handled knife and an extension cord bearing blood-like stains.

Detective Constable Edward Joseph Bibi confirmed documenting the scene through photos, post-mortem imagery, and a video reconstruction.

Members of Dr Biumaitotoya’s family, including his mother, attended the proceedings.

Defence lawyer Losana Taukei told the court that Mr Rokoravolo will testify tomorrow afternoon, running a dual defence of provocation and self-defence.

She submitted that prior events and a specific turning point where Dr Biumaitotoya allegedly attacked the accused triggered the incident.

Mr Rokoravolo plans to call three additional witnesses on Friday.

Deputy Director of Public Prosecutions John Rabuku told the court that relying on both provocation and self-defence is legally impossible, but Ms Taukei maintained she would proceed with both lines.

The prosecution expects to close its case tomorrow following testimony from Lautoka Aspen Hospital Pathologist Dr Pranil Kumar.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Thirty-one families receive keys to new homes
item.description: THIRTY-ONE families from settlements between Navua and Tailevu are now homeowners after receiving the keys to their new houses at the Housing Authority’s Nepani subdivision yesterday. Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka officiated at the handover ceremony, describing the occasion as an important milestone for the families moving into their new homes.
item.content:encoded:
THIRTY-ONE families from settlements between Navua and Tailevu are now homeowners after receiving the keys to their new houses at the Housing Authority’s Nepani subdivision yesterday.

Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka officiated at the handover ceremony, describing the occasion as an important milestone for the families moving into their new homes.

“We want the children to grow up in a good home and safe parenting from father and mother,” Mr Rabuka said.

“It is a great privilege to be here today to hand over the keys to 31 Fijian families who will become proud homeowners here at Housing Authority’s Nepani subdivision.”

Mr Rabuka also revealed that Housing Minister Maciu Nalumisa visited New Zealand to study a housing initiative that repurposes leftover building materials to construct homes for vulnerable communities.

He said the minister returned convinced the model could be adapted for Fiji and that the approach had been incorporated into the new homes.

Mr Nalumisa told this newspaper the project formed part of a broader effort by Government and the Housing Authority to expand home ownership through affordable housing.

“This is part of the government as well as Housing Authority’s initiative to promote home ownership through affordable housing,” he said.

“For a long time at the national level some state housing agencies have been trying to think of strategies to promote ownership.”

Mr Nalumisa said affordability had been a key focus of the development, with the project using technology to reduce housing costs.

He said 28 of the 31 families who received homes had household incomes of less than $30,000 a year, reflecting the program’s focus on lower-income earners.

“In the long term, it’s going to benefit a lot of people in Fiji.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
